STA 141A: Predicting Wildfire Incidence in the United States

Raj Garimella

Abstract

This project builds a month-ahead model to predict whether a notable wildfire (final size ≥ 300 acres) will occur in a given spatial unit in the United States. Using a 100,000-record sample of the national wildfire dataset (1992–2015), I construct a 1° × 1° latitude–longitude grid and aggregate fires to the grid-by-month level. Exploratory analysis shows strong spatial clustering in the western U.S. and pronounced summer seasonality. I fit a baseline logistic regression using only spatial location and month, then a richer logistic regression that adds yearly trend and temporal lags of prior fire incidence. Time-aware validation (rolling origin by year, 2005–2015) shows the lagged model improves average AUC from about 0.72 to 0.73, indicating that recent local history provides modest predictive value beyond static location and seasonality.

Introduction (Optional)

Wildfire risk is highly seasonal and spatially concentrated, yet events remain sporadic at fine spatial resolutions. This report focuses on predicting incidence (whether any notable fire occurs) rather than fire size. The goal is to create a transparent, reproducible modeling pipeline that respects temporal ordering and clearly communicates insights from the data.

1. Data Preprocessing

Notable-fire cutoff

I define notable fires as those with final burned area ≥ 300 acres, following the recommended threshold in the project description. This reduces the heavy class imbalance driven by many very small fires and focuses on operationally meaningful events.

Spatial and temporal units

  • Spatial unit: 1° × 1° latitude–longitude grid cell.
    • I compared 1° and 0.5° grids. The 1° grid yields 1,074 cells and 309,312 cell–months with a notable-fire rate of 0.424%. The 0.5° grid yields 3,389 cells and 976,032 cell–months with a much lower notable-fire rate of 0.138%, creating a sparser outcome. I therefore use the 1° grid to balance spatial resolution and sparsity.
  • Temporal unit: calendar month.

Outcome and predictors

  • Outcome: y = 1 if at least one notable fire occurs in a cell during the month; otherwise 0.
  • Predictors: month (seasonality), latitude bin, longitude bin, year (trend), and lagged incidence in the previous month and previous year.
  • Missing data: latitude and longitude are complete; I do not use county fields because many records have missing county values.
Time span Grid size # grid cells # months Rows (cell-months) Notable-fire rate Predictors
1992 2015 1^0 x 1^0 1074 288 309312 0.424% month, year, lat_bin, lon_bin, lag1, lag12

2. Exploratory Data Analysis

The EDA focuses on spatial clustering and seasonality, both of which guide the choice of predictors.

Spatial pattern: notable fires

Interpretation. Notable fires are concentrated in the western U.S., particularly across the intermountain West and California. This motivates including spatial location as a key predictor.

Temporal pattern: monthly seasonality

Interpretation. Fire incidence peaks in summer months and drops sharply in winter. This supports including month (seasonality) in the baseline model.

3. Modeling

Baseline model

A simple logistic regression uses only static location and seasonal effects:

\[ \text{logit}(P(Y=1)) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \text{(month)} + \beta_2 \text{(lat\_bin)} + \beta_3 \text{(lon\_bin)} \]

This provides an interpretable baseline that captures broad spatial and seasonal patterns.

Model of choice: lagged logistic regression

A richer logistic regression adds year (trend) and temporal lags, capturing short- and medium-term persistence in local fire incidence:

\[ \text{logit}(P(Y=1)) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \text{(month)} + \beta_2 \text{(year)} + \beta_3 \text{(lat\_bin)} + \beta_4 \text{(lon\_bin)} + \beta_5 \text{(lag1)} + \beta_6 \text{(lag12)} \]

I also evaluated a random forest model with the same predictors; its mean AUC over 2005–2015 was about 0.63, which underperformed the lagged logistic model (mean AUC ≈ 0.73). Therefore, I retain the lagged logistic model as the primary non-baseline approach.

4. Model Fitting and Validation

To avoid temporal leakage, I use a rolling-origin evaluation: for each test year from 2005–2015, train on all prior years and evaluate on the held-out year. The primary metric is AUC.

model mean_auc
Baseline logistic 0.722
Lagged logistic 0.732

Interpretation. The lagged logistic model consistently matches or slightly improves on the baseline, with a mean AUC of about 0.73 versus 0.72. The gain is modest but stable, indicating that prior-month and prior-year fire activity contains useful predictive signal.

5. Summary

This project demonstrates that notable wildfires in the United States are highly concentrated in specific regions and seasons, rather than occurring randomly. Through exploratory data analysis, it became clear that fire incidence is strongly seasonal, peaking dramatically in the summer months, and geographically clustered in the western and southwestern United States.

The predictive models confirmed these observations in plain terms: wildfire risk is not just a smooth geographic gradient but is driven by distinct regional hotspots that surge in summer. By allowing latitude and longitude to define those hotspots, the baseline model captured the broad spatial structure of risk.

Adding recent local fire history showed that wildfire risk also has persistence. Areas that experienced a notable fire in the previous month or the previous year remained at elevated risk beyond their usual seasonal and geographic baseline, reinforcing the practical idea that recent fire activity signals heightened near-term vulnerability.

Reproducibility note. GitHub repository link: github.com/rajgarimella/STA141_project.
